description: If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Activate?

Feature Caveat: Activate - If there is no user configuration for DNS servers and HPE Aruba Networking Central is configured, the switch defaults to using Google's DNS server at 8.8.8.8. HPE Aruba Networking Central is enabled by default across all switch platforms.

description: When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Central?

Feature Caveat: Central - When a switch can connect to HPE Aruba Networking Central but is not registered in the inventory or lacks a proper license, it will repeatedly disconnect and reconnect. This cycle will continue until the switch is properly registered or a license is obtained. To avoid this unnecessary reconnection cycle, it is best practice to disable HPE Aruba Networking Central until the switch is registered or a license is obtained.

description: When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Certificates?

Feature Caveat: Certificates - When a switch uses a certificate with a legacy certificate name that is not supported in 10.12 because it contains disallowed characters, the information will migrate properly in the upgrade, but that certificate can no longer be edited. For new certificate names, only alphanumeric characters, dots, dashes, and underscores are allowed.

description: Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Classifiers?
Feature Caveat: Classifiers - Policies containing both MAC and IPv6 classes are not allowed.

description: For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what limitation is mentioned for Classifiers?

Feature Caveat: Classifiers - For secure classifier policy modifications, HPE Aruba Networking strongly encourages a three-step process: bring down the port, make the modifications, and then bring the port back up.

description: No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for CMF?
Feature Caveat: CMF - No other checkpoint besides "startup-configuration" gets migrated during the upgrade process.

description: When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for Hot Patch?

Feature Caveat: Hot Patch - When downloading a hot-patch file using the switch WebUI, log messages may incorrectly state that the file is added to the database with a missing status. This is a temporary state, and will correctly change to Not applied once the download is completed.

description: Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for IP-SLA?

Feature Caveat: IP-SLA - Do not use reserved ports or ports used by other applications/services for different services. When two services use the same port, unexpected behaviors may occur. Best practice is to assign a unique port for each service across the system.

description: Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for RADIUS?
Feature Caveat: RADIUS - Authorization by means of HPE VSAs is not supported.

description: REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for REST?
Feature Caveat: REST - REST supports the 'admin' and 'operator' roles but does not work with TACACS+ command authorization.

description: When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what caveat is documented for SNMP?

Feature Caveat: SNMP - When SNMP is enabled via the switch CLI, it can take between 1-2 minutes for the SNMP daemon to be ready to respond to requests. If a local or external SNMP MIB walk is performed in the interval between when SNMP is first enabled and the SNMP daemon is ready, the MIB walk action will return an error.

description: Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what limitation is mentioned for SNMP?

Feature Caveat: SNMP - Overlay/system partition information is included in HOST-RESOURCES-MIB polling. The overlay partition is a read-only partition and this is reflected in SNMP queries. hrFSAccess.3 = readOnly(2) is expected behavior for overlay partitions. Monitoring tools should not treat this as an error.

description: Symptom: ERSPAN mirroring on 6100/4100i switches fails to capture TX (egress) traffic from remote switches. Only RX traffic and locally-originated TX traffic are mirrored to the ERSPAN destination. ICMP requests from remote switches are missing in the mirrored GRE traffic, while replies are captured correctly. Scenario: This issue occurs when ERSPAN mirroring is configured on a 6100 or 4100i switch and the GRE tunnel egress port is the same as the port where the original packet ingressed. For example, when traffic arrives on port 1/1/3 from a remote switch and needs to be mirrored via GRE tunnel that also exits through port 1/1/3, the mirror fails due to ingress port filtering. Workaround: Connect the ERSPAN tunnel destination (mirror endpoint) via a separate physical port than the mirrored source uplink port, ensuring the tunnel traffic exits through a different port than where the original packet was received.
symptom: ERSPAN mirroring on 6100/4100i switches fails to capture TX (egress) traffic from remote switches. Only RX traffic and locally-originated TX traffic are mirrored to the ERSPAN destination. ICMP requests from remote switches are missing in the mirrored GRE traffic, while replies are captured correctly.
scenario: This issue occurs when ERSPAN mirroring is configured on a 6100 or 4100i switch and the GRE tunnel egress port is the same as the port where the original packet ingressed. workaround: Connect the ERSPAN tunnel destination (mirror endpoint) via a separate physical port than the mirrored source uplink port, ensuring the tunnel traffic exits through a different port than where the original packet was received.

description: Symptom: ACL logging displays incorrect ACE sequence number and action while entries are being updated in hardware. Scenario: This issue occurs when an ACL entry is configured with the logging action the logged output may report an incorrect sequence number and action. Workaround: Wait for ACL programming to finish before utilizing logs for debug/analysis of traffic.
symptom: ACL logging displays incorrect ACE sequence number and action while entries are being updated in hardware.
scenario: This issue occurs when an ACL entry is configured with the logging action the logged output may report an incorrect sequence number and action.
workaround: Wait for ACL programming to finish before utilizing logs for debug/analysis of traffic.

description: Symptom: MACs are only showing on one side of a VSX pair. For instance, a MAC is learned on a physical port on one VSX device and is missing from the peer VSX device. Scenario: This issue can occur after resolving a network loop which causes the MACs to quickly move from one VSX device to the other, particularly when MACs are moving from EVPN tunnels to physical ports. Workaround: Using the CLI to clear the problematic MAC address.

description: Symptom: hpe-restd crashes when attempting to obtain Central location from Activate. Scenario: This issue may occur if, the TLS handshake when connecting to Activate fails and causes a crash on the hpe-restd daemon (where Central connectivity resides). Workaround: DIsable the feature where Central is not being used and the crash is occuring.
symptom: hpe-restd crashes when attempting to obtain Central location from Activate.
scenario: This issue may occur if, the TLS handshake when connecting to Activate fails and causes a crash on the hpe-restd daemon (where Central connectivity resides).
workaround: DIsable the feature where Central is not being used and the crash is occuring.

scenario: This issue may occur if, the TLS handshake when connecting to Activate fails and causes a crash on the hpe-restd daemon (where Central connectivity resides).
workaround: DIsable the feature where Central is not being used and the crash is occuring.

Central Connectivity (Bug ID 412351): The documented workaround is: DIsable the feature where Central is not being used and the crash is occuring.

description: Symptom: Port flapping with port-access authentication cycling and causes STP instability. Ports continuously transition between blocking/forwarding states. LLDP PVID mismatches cause neighbor relationship instability and device profile classification failures. Scenario: This issue may occur when device-profile authentication is enabled with LLDP-based switch classification and port security + STP is enabled.
symptom: Port flapping with port-access authentication cycling and causes STP instability. Ports continuously transition between blocking/forwarding states. LLDP PVID mismatches cause neighbor relationship instability and device profile classification failures.
scenario: This issue may occur when device-profile authentication is enabled with LLDP-based switch classification and port security + STP is enabled.

description: Symptom: Classifier Policy is not enforced for traffic coming from an ISL link. Scenario: For a topology with VSX+ VRRP, if the policy is applied on SVI, all packets which reach the VRRP secondary device go to the primary device via the ISL. All of those packets are not getting classifier policy enforced. Even though the policy action is PBR redirect, those packets are not getting redirected. Workaround: Two workarounds are possible to prevent packets from being forwarded to VSX primary via ISL. 1. Use active gateway to give better active active redundancy. Packets reaching either VSX device process the packet destined to active gateway. 2. Turn off the interface connection from VRRP secondary device to down switch. This method may cause redundancy and is not preferred.

section: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
task: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
procedure: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy).
Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config.
Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.
description: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?

Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.
description: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

messages.content: For 6000 AOS-CX 10.16.1040, what is the manual configuration restore procedure for software downgrade?

Downgrade Restore: To restore configuration after a software downgrade, follow the documented procedure in order: 1. Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, PL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

procedure: Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.

Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.

When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
